1: The introduction to the lament over Tyre as a ship is a simple date-and-call formula — but the form matters. The lament (qinah) is the dirge-form, the song of the dead. Ezekiel sings the death-song over Tyre before it has happened — the prophetic perfect. Christ employs the same prophetic mourning in Luke 19:41-44, weeping over Jerusalem before its destruction and describing its fate in prophetic past tense. The prophet who sings the dirge over the living city is like Christ who announces the coming judgment while simultaneously grieving it.
2: “Raise a lamentation over Tyre” — the call to lamentation places the prophet in the role of mourner-on-behalf-of-the-doomed. Ezekiel does not celebrate Tyre's fall but grieves it. This is the prophetic pathos that Heschel identified as the distinctive mark of Israelite prophecy: the prophet shares YHWH's grief over the necessity of judgment. Christ embodies this pathos most fully: he is simultaneously the judge who pronounces the verdict and the one who weeps over those condemned (John 11:35; Luke 19:41-44). The God who judges is the God who mourns.
3: Tyre's self-assessment: “I am perfect in beauty” (ani kelilat yofi) — the same claim Ezekiel directs ironically against Jerusalem in Ezekiel 16:14 (“your beauty was perfect through my splendor that I had bestowed on you”). Tyre claims inherent beauty; Jerusalem's beauty was derived. Christ alone is inherently “perfect in beauty” — Psalms 45:2: “you are the most handsome of the sons of men; grace is poured upon your lips.” The beauty that Tyre claimed for itself and Jerusalem received as a gift is actually Christ's own — the beautiful one from whom all creaturely beauty derives.
4: “Your builders have perfected your beauty” — Tyre's beauty was constructed, engineered, designed. The builders who perfected the ship's beauty are human craftsmen. Christ's beauty, by contrast, is uncreated: he is the “radiance of the glory of God and the exact imprint of his nature” (Hebrews 1:3) — not constructed but eternally generated. The ship's beauty will eventually be wrecked (Ezekiel 27:26); the beauty of the incarnate Son is permanent and the foundation of the new creation's glory (Revelation 21:11).
5: The ship's planks from Senir (the Amorite name for Hermon, Deuteronomy 3:9) and the mast from Lebanon cedars — the finest available timber. The cedar of Lebanon was the premier building material for temples (1 Kings 5:6-10), including Solomon's temple. Christ is the true temple (John 2:19-21) — the place where YHWH dwells — and the cedars that Tyre used for commerce, Solomon used for worship, and Ezekiel uses as an image of Assyrian imperial pride (Ezekiel 31), are the raw material of that which points to the divine dwelling in human flesh.
6: Oaks of Bashan and Cyprus ivory for the deck — the best materials from the east and west. The Bashan oaks were famous for their strength (Isaiah 2:13; Zechariah 11:2); Bashan is also associated with the Rephaim/giants and is a biblical symbol of formidable earthly strength. In Christ's kingship, he who “descended to the lower parts of the earth” (Ephesians 4:9 — to the realm where the Bashan-strength had no power over the dead) is the true master of all materials and domains.
7: Egyptian linen for the sail, purple and blue from Elishah (possibly Cyprus or the Aegean) for the awning — the finest luxury textiles. The purple and blue were the colors of the tabernacle curtains (Exodus 26:1) and the high-priestly garments (Exodus 28:4-6). Christ was clothed in purple by the soldiers in mockery (John 19:2) — unknowingly robing the High Priest in the colors of the sanctuary. The purple that commercial Tyre used for beauty, the tabernacle used for holiness, and the soldiers used for mockery, becomes in Revelation 19:13-16 the robe of Christ the conquering King.
8: Sidon and Arvad as oarsmen — skilled Phoenician sailors directing the ship. The skilled navigation of Tyre's commercial enterprise draws on the whole Phoenician maritime tradition. Christ calls fishermen (Matthew 4:18-22) — not navigators of commerce but workers in the ancient subsistence economy — to be “fishers of men.” The contrast between Tyre's commercial pilots and Christ's fishing-disciples is the contrast between the economy of acquisition and the economy of grace.
9: “All the ships of the sea with their sailors were in you to trade your merchandise” — the universal commerce flowing to Tyre. In Christ, the nations flow not to a commercial hub but to a person: John 12:32: “I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself.” The attraction of the cross replaces the attraction of the market — and the “all” who are drawn to Christ exceeds the “all the ships” that traded at Tyre.
10: Persian, Lydian, and Libyan soldiers in Tyre's army — a multiethnic mercenary force. The military power that Tyre's wealth could buy was formidable. Christ's army in Revelation 19:14 is “the armies of heaven, wearing fine linen, white and clean” — not mercenaries bought with commercial wealth but the redeemed clothed in Christ's righteousness. The contrast between Tyre's bought soldiers and Christ's redeemed army is the difference between a commercial transaction and a covenant relationship.
11: Arvad soldiers on the walls, Gammadim in the towers — military perfection, shields covering every surface, beauty made perfect through armament. Christ's armor is described in Ephesians 6:10-17, but it is fundamentally different: not bronze shields on a commercial ship's walls but the armor of truth, righteousness, peace, faith, and salvation. The defense of Tyre was its military hardware; the defense of Christ's people is his character and work applied to them.
12: Tarshish trading silver, iron, tin, and lead — the metals of ancient commerce. Tarshish is associated with the far west (possibly Spain or Sardinia) and represents the farthest reaches of the known trading world. The metals of Tarshish are raw materials for industry and weapons. In Christ, the metals are transformed: the “silver” of redemption (1 Peter 1:18: “you were ransomed ... not with perishable things such as silver or gold, but with the precious blood of Christ”) exceeds all commercial metals. The ransoming of humanity cost more than Tyre's entire treasury.
13: “They traded human beings and vessels of bronze in your markets” — Meshech and Tubal in slave trade and bronze. The commodification of human beings in the ancient commercial system is explicitly named. Christ's redemption is the direct reversal of the slave trade: he purchased people “for God from every tribe and language and people and nation” (Revelation 5:9) — but not to own them as slaves but to make them “a kingdom and priests to our God.” The commerce that traded humans as commodities is overthrown by the One who paid for humans to be liberated into royal priesthood.
14: Beth-togarmah with horses, war horses, and mules — the cavalry-trade from Armenia (Togarmah = Armenia in later tradition). The horses of war supplied by Togarmah represent military power purchasable through commerce. Christ enters Jerusalem on a humble donkey (Matthew 21:5; Zechariah 9:9), deliberately contrasting his arrival with the war-horse economy. The King who comes to Tyre is bought with Togarmah's war horses; the King who comes to Zion comes in humility. Revelation 19:11 reverses this: Christ comes on a white horse — but the horse is not purchased through commerce; it is the mount of divine judgment.
15: Dedan and the coastlands with ivory and ebony — luxury goods from Arabia and Africa. The ivory and ebony represent the exotic beauty that wealth could procure from the ends of the earth. The Magi who came to the infant Jesus brought “gold and frankincense and myrrh” (Matthew 2:11) — luxury goods from the east, echoing the tribute of the nations flowing to Zion that Isaiah envisioned (Isaiah 60:5-6). What flowed to Tyre as commercial profit flows to Christ as worship: the wealth of the nations becomes the offering of the nations.
16: Syria with emeralds, purple, embroidered cloth, fine linen, coral, and rubies — the luxury goods of the Levantine trade. These are the same materials used in the tabernacle and priestly vestments: purple, embroidered cloth, and fine linen are the vocabulary of the sanctuary. Everything Tyre used for commercial display, the tabernacle used for divine service. Christ is the fulfillment of the tabernacle-pattern (John 1:14: “the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us”) — the sanctuary-materials that commercial empire appropriated are restored to their proper function as the dwelling-place of divine glory.
17: “Judah and the land of Israel were your traders; they traded wheat from Minnith, cakes, honey, olive oil, and balm” — Israel among Tyre's trading partners, supplying agricultural products. The covenant people are embedded in the commercial system of the nations. This is the diagnosis that the prophets repeatedly make: Israel's sin is not only idolatry but the commercialization of covenant life. Christ's cleansing of the temple (John 2:16: “Stop making my Father's house a house of trade”) is the definitive purification of the covenant community from Tyre's commercial spirit.
18: Damascus with wine and wool — the agricultural luxury products of Syria. The extensive merchant catalog (Ezekiel 27:12-24) is the OT's most detailed picture of ancient international commerce, mapping the trading world from Spain to India. It is the direct source for Rev 18's Babylon-lament catalog (Ezekiel 27:12-13) which lists 28 categories of luxury goods. The point of both catalogs is the same: all the world's material wealth, assembled and organized by human commercial genius, will be swept away in a moment. Only what Christ builds — his church (Matthew 16:18) — will stand.
19: Dan and Javan (Greece) from Uzal with wrought iron, cassia, and fragrant calamus — iron for tools and weapons, spices for luxury use. The cassia and calamus are ingredients in the holy anointing oil of the tabernacle (Exodus 30:23-25). The spices that flowed through Tyre's markets for commercial purchase were also the sacred ingredients of covenant worship. Christ's anointing with costly nard (Mark 14:3-9) is the NT equivalent: “wherever the gospel is proclaimed in the whole world, what she has done will be told in memory of her.” The commercial spice-trade is transformed into an act of devotion.
20: Dedan with saddlecloths for riding — equipment for horses and camels, the transportation infrastructure of ancient trade. The saddlecloths represent the practical underside of luxury commerce: the logistics, the animals, the equipment. Christ entered Jerusalem on a borrowed donkey with cloaks spread on the road (Matthew 21:7-8) — the simplest possible version of royal transportation. The king who owned all the saddlecloths of Dedan came to his city in the humility that the commercial mind cannot recognize as royal.
21: Arabia and Kedar with lambs, rams, and goats — the pastoral economy of the desert peoples. The lambs and rams connect to the sacrificial system: Kedar's flocks supplied both commerce and, potentially, temple sacrifices. Christ is “the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world” (John 1:29) — the One to whom all the lambs of commercial and sacrificial trade ultimately pointed. The lambs traded through Tyre's markets were commercial commodities; the Lamb of God is the one whose sacrifice ends all commercial and sacrificial transactions.
22: “The merchants of Sheba and Raamah traded with you; they traded in the finest of all spices, all kinds of precious stones, and gold” — Sheba (southern Arabia / Ethiopia) with spices, precious stones, and gold echoes the Queen of Sheba's tribute to Solomon (1 Kings 10:2, 10: “she gave the king 120 talents of gold, large quantities of spices, and precious stones”) and anticipates the Magi's gifts to the infant Jesus (Matthew 2:11: “they presented gifts of gold and frankincense and myrrh”). The wealth of nations flowing to YHWH's anointed king is a recurring pattern: Tyre monetizes it for commerce; Jesus receives it as eschatological tribute.
23: Haran, Canneh, Eden, Asshur, Chilmad — trade partners from Mesopotamia and northern Syria. The geographic extent of Tyre's trading network spans from Spain (Tarshish, Ezekiel 27:12) to India (possibly Dedan's eastern connections) and from north Africa to Mesopotamia. This global commerce is the pre-modern world's version of what Paul describes as the principalities and powers ruling through “the elementary principles of the world” (Galatians 4:3; Colossians 2:8): the organized systems of human commerce that bind people to the material world. Christ's liberation from these systems (Colossians 2:15: “disarming the rulers and authorities ... triumphing over them in the cross”) is the defeat of the commercial empire behind every Tyre.
24: “Choice garments, in blue and embroidered cloth, in chests of fine clothing bound with cords” — the luxury clothing trade. Clothing in the OT carries theological weight: the garments of Adam and Eve (Genesis 3:21, YHWH provides clothing after the fall), the high-priestly garments (Exod 28), the robe of righteousness (Isaiah 61:10), the filthy garments of Joshua the high priest (Zechariah 3:3-5). Christ's seamless tunic (John 19:23-24, soldiers cast lots for it citing Psalms 22:18) is the NT clothing-symbol: the garment that cannot be divided, the covering that surpasses all Tyre's blue and embroidered robes.
25: “The ships of Tarshish carried your merchandise; you were filled and made very glorious in the heart of the seas” — Tyre at its peak: “filled and very glorious.” The glory of the commercial empire at its apex is the prelude to its destruction (Ezekiel 27:26). The pattern of “fullness leading to fall” is the prophetic diagnosis of Tyre, Assyria, Babylon, and ultimately of every self-sufficient civilization. Christ's fullness (pleroma) is the opposite: “in him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily” (Colossians 2:9) — not the fullness of accumulated commercial wealth but the fullness of divine being, which cannot be wrecked by any east wind.
26: “The east wind has wrecked you in the heart of the seas” — the east wind is YHWH's instrument of judgment throughout the OT: the east wind dried up the Red Sea (Exodus 14:21), brought the plague of locusts (Exodus 10:13), destroyed Jonah's shade (Jonah 4:8), and scattered Israel (Psalms 48:7). Tyre's merchant ship is wrecked by the same wind that YHWH wields in covenant judgment. Christ, who “rebuked the wind and the raging waves” (Luke 8:24), is the master of the east wind that wrecks Tyre — the same power that judges is the power that stills the storm for his people.
27: “All will sink into the heart of the seas on the day of your ruin” — the totality of the sinking is emphasized: wealth, merchandise, goods, mariners, pilots, caulkers, dealers, soldiers — everything. The all-encompassing loss echoes the rich man in Luke 12:20: “You fool! This night your soul is required of you, and the things you have prepared, whose will they be?” The day of ruin comes suddenly, and nothing of the commercial structure survives. Only what is built on Christ — “a stone of testing, a precious cornerstone, a sure foundation” (Isaiah 28:16) — survives the wreck.
28: “At the sound of the cry of your pilots the countryside shakes” — the lament of the skilled navigators causes the land itself to tremble. The “shaking of the earth” motif in prophetic texts is regularly associated with divine judgment and theophany (Isaiah 2:21; Ezekiel 13:13; Haggai 2:6-7; Hebrews 12:26-27). The cry of Tyre's pilots — the most skilled, the highest-paid, the most celebrated — signals the collapse of human expertise before the divine judgment. “I will destroy the wisdom of the wise, and the discernment of the discerning I will thwart” (1 Corinthians 1:19, citing Isaiah 29:14) — Christ crucified is “foolishness to Greeks” (1 Corinthians 1:23), the cosmic rebuke of all human navigational skill.
29: “All who handle the oar — the mariners and all the pilots of the sea — will come down from their ships and stand on the shore” — the maritime community's abandonment of their vessels in mourning for Tyre. They stand on solid ground having left the sea — the instability of the commercial enterprise becomes apparent only in the moment of catastrophic loss. Christ invites his disciples to come down from their boats: Peter walks on the water (Matthew 14:29-30), then sinks, then is caught by Christ. The invitation to leave the commercial sea and stand on solid ground is the gospel's call.
30: “They will raise their voices over you and cry out bitterly; they will throw dust on their heads and roll in ashes” — the mourning rituals of the ancient world: dust on the head, rolling in ashes. These are the gestures of the deepest grief (Job 2:12-13; Lamentations 3:29; Joshua 7:6). The mourning that the nations perform over Tyre is a parody of genuine repentance: they grieve the loss of their commercial partner, not their sin. Christ's call is to genuine mourning over sin (Matthew 5:4: “blessed are those who mourn, for they shall be comforted”) — the sorrow that leads to repentance, not merely the grief of material loss.
31: Shaved heads and sackcloth for Tyre — the mourning rituals that in the prophets belong to genuine lament for sin (Joel 1:13; Isaiah 22:12). The merchants perform the form of mourning without the substance. Christ's beatitude (Matthew 5:4) and Paul's distinction (2 Corinthians 7:10: “godly grief produces repentance leading to salvation, but worldly grief produces death”) draws exactly this line: the grief over Tyre's fall is worldly grief, producing nothing but despair. The grief over one's sin before Christ is godly grief, producing repentance and life.
32: “Who was ever destroyed like Tyre, like one silenced in the midst of the sea?” — the rhetorical question of Tyre's uniqueness. The same question was asked of Babylon in Jeremiah 50:23 (“how the hammer of the whole earth has been cut down and broken!”) and Revelation 18:18 (“What city was like the great city?”). The lament over unprecedented destruction is itself a theological claim: YHWH's judgment is uniquely comprehensive, not like ordinary military defeat. Christ's cross is also unprecedented: “Has it ever happened in all your days or in the days of your fathers? Tell your children about it, and let your children tell their children” (Joel 1:2-3) — the singular, unrepeatable event at the center of history.
33: “When your merchandise went out over the seas, you satisfied many peoples; with your great wealth and goods you enriched the kings of the earth” — Tyre's positive contribution to the ancient world is acknowledged: it genuinely satisfied and enriched. This is not merely hypocritical praise; Tyre's commercial network did create real prosperity for many. The judgment is not that commercial activity is evil but that Tyre claimed ultimate credit and status for what YHWH provided. Christ satisfies far more comprehensively than Tyre: “I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst” (John 6:35) — the satisfaction that Tyre's trade offered temporarily, Christ offers permanently.
34: “Now you are wrecked by the seas, in the depths of the waters; your merchandise and all your company have sunk with you” — the wrecking is complete. The “depths of the waters” (maamaqqim mayim) recalls Psalms 69:2 (“I have come into deep waters, and the flood sweeps over me”), the lament of the righteous sufferer — whose words Christ quotes from the cross (Psalms 69:21 in John 19:28-29). The depths that swallow Tyre are the same depths that Christ entered in his Passion — but from the depths Christ rose, and from those depths he raises his people (Psalms 130:1: “Out of the depths I cry to you, O LORD”).
35: “All the inhabitants of the coastlands are appalled at you; their kings shudder with horror and their faces are convulsed with fear” — the face-convulsion of the kings at Tyre's fall echoes the suffering servant's effect: “so his appearance was marred beyond human semblance, and his form beyond that of the children of mankind — so shall he sprinkle many nations; kings shall shut their mouths because of him” (Isaiah 52:14-15). The horror that Tyre's fall produces in kings is matched by the awestruck silence that the servant's suffering and exaltation produces. Both the fall of commercial empire and the exaltation of the suffering servant silence kings.
36: “The merchants among the peoples hiss at you; you have come to a horrible end and will be no more forever” — the merchants' hissing (sharak, a gesture of contemptuous horror) at Tyre's fall is echoed in Lamentations 2:15 (“all who pass your way hiss and wag their heads at the daughter of Jerusalem”) and in the crowd at the crucifixion (Matthew 27:39: “those who passed by derided him, wagging their heads”). The same contemptuous gesture that the merchants direct at fallen Tyre was directed at Christ on the cross. What looks like the hissing at ultimate failure is, in Christ, the moment of ultimate victory — the moment when the world's contempt meets its match in the Father's vindication through resurrection.
